title: Iron as an after-bath modifier in natural dyeing
subtitle: Using iron sulfate after the dye bath to shift and sadden colour on mordanted fibre
excerpt: Iron sulfate used after the dye bath is called an iron modifier or iron saddle. It shifts yellows and greens toward olive and khaki, deepens oranges toward rust, and generally saddens or darkens the dye colour. The iron modifier bath is separate from the dye bath and is used on already-dyed, alum-mordanted fibre. A very small quantity of iron is enough: over-ironing breaks down wool fibre.
sourceNotes: Iron as a dye modifier is a documented natural dyeing practice. Safety guidance follows contemporary dyeing instruction.

content.text: bath shifts the colour of dyed fibre after it leaves the dye pot. The
effect dulls and deepens the colour: a bright weld yellow becomes gold or olive; a madder orange becomes rust or brown. This

technique lets a single dye recipe produce two colour outcomes from the same batch. Use the minimum iron quantity: 1 to 2 percent weight of fibre (WOF) is usually sufficient. More iron damages wool fibre.

attrs.body: Wear gloves. Iron sulfate irritates eyes and skin. Neutralise waste water with baking soda before disposal.
attrs.heading: Safety

items.name: Already-dyed, alum-mordanted wool or protein fibre

content.text: Weigh out 1 to 2 g of iron sulfate per 100 g of fibre. Dissolve in a small amount of hot water in the mordant jar before adding to the modifier pot. Iron sulfate does not dissolve well in cold water and undissolved crystals can spot the fibre.
Fill the dye pot with warm water, enough to allow the fibre to move freely. Add the dissolved iron solution and stir. The water will turn faintly amber or yellow-green. Bring the bath to 50 to 60°C.

Wet out the dyed fibre in warm water before entering the modifier bath. Gently submerge the fibre and leave at 50 to 60°C for 15 to 20 minutes, turning occasionally. The colour shift should be visible within the first few minutes. Remove when the desired tone is reached.

Rinse the fibre thoroughly in water at the same temperature as the modifier bath. Follow with a light wool wash rinse. Squeeze out excess water and dry flat. Dispose of the modifier bath by neutralising with baking soda (add gradually until fizzing stops) before pouring down the drain.

attrs.intro: Two common problems with iron modification.

items.fix: Keep iron at 1 to 2 percent WOF. Remove fibre as soon as the colour shift is visible. Iron continues to affect fibre after removal until rinsed.
items.cause: Too much iron sulfate was used, or the fibre was left in the bath too long.
items.symptom: Fibre feels harsh or brittle after the modifier bath

items.fix: Always pre-dissolve in hot water. Always wet out fibre in warm water before the modifier bath.
items.cause: Iron sulfate crystals were not fully dissolved before entering the bath, or the fibre was not wet out before entering.

attrs.heading: Common problems
